4: ʾîš bᵉḥeṭʾô — 'each person for his own sin' (25:4). The Chronicler does not paraphrase but quotes Deut 24:16 verbatim as the legal ground for Amaziah's refusal to execute the sons of his father's killers. The formula establishes individual moral accountability as a statutory principle rooted in the Torah: fathers shall not die for children, children shall not die for fathers. The Aramaic cognate tradition preserves this non-transference principle in Targum as well. Ezekiel 18 develops the same formula into a full theology; the NT picks it up in Rom 14:12 ('each of us will give an account of himself to God'). The Chronicler's explicit citation — unusual in the historical narratives — signals that the legal basis mattered to the story's meaning, not merely the outcome.
15: ʾăšer lōʾ-hiṣṣîlû ʾet-ʿammām — 'who did not deliver their own people from your hand' (25:15). The divine messenger's rhetorical question deploys the futility-of-idolatry argument in its sharpest form: you are seeking gods who demonstrably failed their own worshippers when you defeated them. The argument appears in Isa 10:10-11, 37:12, and elsewhere — defeated gods are gods who cannot save. The Chronicler uses military outcome as theological verdict: divine power is measured by actual capacity to deliver. The irony is structural — Amaziah, who just won a battle (vv. 11-12), immediately adopts the gods of the losers.
16: hᵃyōʾēṣ lammeleḵ nᵉtanûkā — 'have they appointed you as the king's counselor?' (25:16). The prophet's sharp counter-question when Amaziah silences him. The yāʾaṣ / counselor vocabulary is significant: 1 Chr 27:32 catalogs David's counselors; the Chronicler regards unsolicited prophetic counsel as divinely mandated, not an imposition. Amaziah's refusal to receive the word — and his mocking it — triggers the Chronicler's editorial formula: 'God has determined to destroy you, because you have done this and have not listened to my counsel.' The prophet's withdrawal from the conversation does not withdraw the divine verdict.
1: ʾămatsyāhû — 'Amaziah' (25:1). The name means 'YHWH is strong/mighty' (ʾāmaṣ + Yah). The accession formula — age 25, reigns 29 years in Jerusalem, mother Jehoaddin of Jerusalem — follows the Chronicler's standard regnal introduction. The Chronicler draws Amaziah's reign primarily from 2 Kings 14:1-20, but reshapes it around the dāraš-seeking pattern: Amaziah's half-heartedness (v2) predicts his ultimate downfall.
2: wəlōʾ bəlēḇāḇ šālēm — 'but not with a complete heart' (25:2). The qualification of Amaziah's 'doing right in YHWH's eyes' is the Chronicler's pivotal moral diagnosis. lēḇāḇ šālēm (whole/complete heart) is the Chronicler's standard of full covenant fidelity (cf. 1 Chr 28:9; 2 Chr 15:17; 16:9 — 'YHWH's eyes range through the whole earth to strengthen those whose heart is complete toward him'). Half-heartedness is not neutrality; it is the disposition that leads to the idolatry of v14.
3: wayyahărōg ʾet-ʿăḇāḏāyw hamməkîm ʾet-hamelek ʾāḇîw — 'he killed his servants who had murdered his father the king' (25:3). The execution of the regicides follows the establishment of royal authority (hēḵîn hammamlāḵāh, when the kingdom was firm to him). The Chronicler validates this act and immediately in v4 cites the legal principle that prevents the sons from also being executed — an unusual legislative citation mid-narrative, marking Amaziah's early law-observance before his later apostasy.
5: wayyiqbōṣ ʾet-yəhûḏāh wayyaʿămîḏēm — 'he mustered Judah and organized them' (25:5). The military census by ancestral houses (lēḇêt ʾăḇôtêhem) is the Chronicler's standard conscription-and-organization formula. The result — 300,000 choice men from Judah and Benjamin, age 20 and above — establishes the tribal military strength before introducing the Israelite mercenaries of v6. The Chronicler's detail ('spear and shield') establishes their combat readiness, making the prophet's warning (vv7-8) all the more costly to heed.
6: wayyiśkōr miyyiśrāʾēl mēʾāh ʾelep gibbôr ḥāyil bəmēʾāh kikkār-kāsep — 'He hired a hundred thousand soldiers of proven valor from Israel for a hundred talents of silver' (25:6). The hired-soldier (śākar) transaction at 100 talents is the economic baseline against which the prophet's reassurance of v9 operates ('YHWH can give you much more than this'). The Chronicler's introduction of this detail — Israel mercenaries for pay — sets up the theological tension: paid soldiers from the northern apostate kingdom are not YHWH's army.
7: ʾîš hāʾĕlōhîm bāʾ ʾēlāyw lēʾmōr hammelek ʾal-yēṣēʾ ʿimməkā ṣəḇā yiśrāʾēl kî ʾên YHWH ʿim-yiśrāʾēl — 'a man of God came to him saying: O king, do not let the army of Israel go with you, for YHWH is not with Israel' (25:7). ʾîš hāʾĕlōhîm (man of God) is the Chronicler's designation for prophetic intermediaries who deliver direct divine oracles. The oracle hinges on ʾên YHWH ʿim-yiśrāʾēl — YHWH's absence from the northern kingdom is stated as a theological fact, not a polemic. Military alliance with those YHWH has abandoned imports their failure.
8: kî ʾim-lāḇôʾ ʿăśēh ḥăzaq lammlāḥāmāh yakkîlkā hāʾĕlōhîm lipenê ʾôyēḇ kî yēš-kōaḥ bēʾlōhîm laʿăzōr wəlahhakšîl — 'But if you go and fight bravely, God will make you fall before your enemy, for God has power to help or to cause to fall' (25:8). The theological axiom is the Chronicler's military theology condensed: yēš-kōaḥ bēʾlōhîm laʿăzōr wəlahhakšîl (God has power both to help and to bring low). Military capability is irrelevant; alignment with God determines outcome. This principle governs every battle-scene in Chronicles.
9: wayyōʾmer ʾămatsyāhû ləʾîš hāʾĕlōhîm ûmāh-laʿăśôt lammēʾāh hakkikār ʾăšer nāṯattî ləgəḏûḏ yiśrāʾēl wayyōʾmer ʾîš hāʾĕlōhîm yēš-laYHWH lātet ləkā harbēh mizzeh — 'Amaziah asked the man of God: But what about the hundred talents I gave to the Israelite troops? The man of God replied: YHWH can give you much more than this' (25:9). The economic reasoning ('I've already paid') is deflected by a theological principle: YHWH's compensatory capacity exceeds any financial loss. The Chronicler places this exchange to mark Amaziah's obedience — dismissing the troops despite the loss demonstrates genuine trust. Cf. the pattern of costly obedience yielding greater reward (2 Chr 20:20).
10: wayyabdîlēm ʾămatsyāhû laggəḏûḏ ʾăšer-bāʾ ʾēlāyw mēʾeprayim lālaket limqômām wayyiḥar-ʾappām məʾōd bîhûḏāh — 'Amaziah dismissed the Israelite force and sent them home. They were enraged against Judah and returned home in fierce anger' (25:10). The dismissal creates the precondition for v13's retaliatory raid: the angry soldiers, deprived of plunder and pay, attack Judean cities on their way home. The Chronicler presents this consequence without moralizing — it is the cost of obedience, not its failure. YHWH's oracle came true about the danger of Israelite alliance, even after compliance.
11: waʾămatsyāhû hiṯḥāzzeq wayyānaḥ ʾet-ʿammô wayyēlek gêʾ-hammelaḥ wayyak ʾet-bənê śēʿîr ʿăśārāh ʾălāpîm — 'Amaziah took courage, led his people to the Valley of Salt, and struck down ten thousand men of Seir' (25:11). hiṯḥāzzeq (strengthened himself/took courage) is the Chronicler's reflexive intensification verb for bold action under divine confidence. The Valley of Salt (gêʾ hammelaḥ) is the same site as 2 Sam 8:13 and 2 Kgs 14:7 — a historically documented Edomite battleground south of the Dead Sea. The military success validates the prophet's assurance.
12: waʿăśārāh ʾălāpîm ḥayyîm šāḇû bənê yəhûḏāh wayyōlîkûm lərōʾš haśśelaʿ wayyišlîkûm mērōʾš haśśelaʿ wayyiṯnappəṣû kullām — 'The Judahites also captured ten thousand alive, brought them to the top of the rock, and threw them all down so that they were all dashed to pieces' (25:12). This atrocity — unique to Chronicles, not in 2 Kings — is narrated without condemnation, which makes its presence disturbing. The Chronicler does not editorially endorse it; the narrative placement between v11's victory and v14's idolatry suggests the brutal treatment of Edomites prefigures Amaziah's spiritual brutality in adopting their gods.
13: ûbənê haggəḏûḏ ʾăšer hēšîḇ ʾămatsyāhû millālaket ʿimmô lammilḥāmāh wayyipšəṭû bəʿārê yəhûḏāh — 'The soldiers Amaziah had dismissed raided the cities of Judah from Samaria to Beth-horon, struck down three thousand, and took much plunder' (25:13). The dismissed Israelite mercenaries' revenge raid is the Chronicler's inserted consequence: Amaziah obeyed the prophet (good), but the act's ripple effects still cost Judah 3,000 lives and much plunder. This nuanced presentation prevents the Chronicler's theology from becoming simplistic reward-and-punishment: obedience is costly, but YHWH's command remains right.
14: wayhî ʾaḥărê-ḇôʾ ʾămatsyāhû mēhakkôt ʾet-ʾĕdômîm wayyāḇēʾ ʾet-ʾĕlōhê bənê-śēʿîr wayyaʿămîḏēm lô lēʾlōhîm — 'After Amaziah returned from defeating the Edomites, he brought back the gods of the men of Seir, set them up as his gods, and worshipped before them' (25:14). The post-victory idolatry is the Chronicler's most devastating irony: Amaziah conquers the people whose religion he then adopts. wayyaʿămîḏēm lô lēʾlōhîm (set them up as gods for himself) — he made the gods of a defeated nation his own. The gods that could not save their worshippers from Judah are now Judah's gods. The logical absurdity is the point.
17: wayyiggāmer ʾămatsyāhû melek-yəhûḏāh wayyišlaḥ ʾel-yôʾāš ben-yəhôʾāḥāz ben-yēhûʾ melek yiśrāʾēl lēʾmōr lěkāh nitrāʾeh pānîm — 'Then Amaziah king of Judah took counsel and sent to Joash king of Israel: Come, let us face each other' (25:17). nitrāʾeh pānîm (let us see each other's faces) is the challenge idiom for open battle. The Chronicler adds 'took counsel' (wayyiggāmer) compared to 2 Kings 14:8, suggesting Amaziah deliberated — and chose pride over prudence. His Egyptian-prophet-like spiritual overreach (after idolatry) now manifests as military overreach.
18: wayyišlaḥ yôʾāš melek yiśrāʾēl ʾel-ʾămatsyāhû melek-yəhûḏāh lēʾmōr haḥôaḥ ʾăšer ballāḇānôn šālaḥ ʾel-hāʾereẓ ʾăšer ballāḇānôn lēʾmōr tən-ʾet-bittəkā lišəlîlî — 'Joash king of Israel sent to Amaziah king of Judah: The thornbush in Lebanon sent to the cedar in Lebanon: Give your daughter to my son in marriage' (25:18). The thistle-and-cedar fable is diplomatic condescension: the thornbush (Amaziah/Judah) presumes to negotiate with the cedar (Joash/Israel). The marriage-request parody exposes the absurdity of the challenge — Amaziah has just committed idolatry and the Chronicler implies that Joash's fable is accurate even if his character is not exemplary.
19: ʾāmartā hikkîtî ʾet-ʾĕdôm ûnəśāʾăkā libbəkā lahhikkābēd — 'You think: I have defeated Edom, and your heart lifts you up to boast' (25:19). nāśāʾ lēḇ (the heart lifts up) — the pride-elevation formula; identical to Uzziah's gāḇah libbô (26:16) and Hezekiah's later pride (32:25). The Chronicler returns repeatedly to the dynamic of military success producing spiritual arrogance. Joash's diagnosis is theologically accurate even though he delivers it as diplomatic manipulation.
20: wəʾămatsyāhû lōʾ šāmaʿ kî min-hāʾĕlōhîm hîʾ — 'But Amaziah did not listen, for it was from God' (25:20). The Chronicler's theological explanation for Amaziah's stubbornness is unusual: God ordained his non-listening. The Hebrew phrase min-hāʾĕlōhîm hîʾ (it was from God) places divine sovereignty behind the king's bad choice — not to exculpate Amaziah but to show that his idolatry (v14) has placed him outside the space of wise counsel. God's judicial hardening is the response to deliberate covenant abandonment (cf. Rom 1:24-28).
21: wayyaʿal yôʾāš melek yiśrāʾēl wayyitrāʾû pānîm hûʾ wə-ʾămatsyāhû melek-yəhûḏāh bəbêt-šemeš ʾăšer lîhûḏāh — 'Joash king of Israel went up, and they faced each other at Beth-shemesh, which belongs to Judah' (25:21). The battle location is theologically significant: Beth-shemesh ('house of the sun') is in Judah's own territory (Josh 15:10; 21:16), meaning the defeat happens on home ground. Judah is invaded by the northern kingdom — the same people whose mercenaries (v13) had already raided Judah. The battle is lost before it is fought.
22: wayyinnāgep yəhûḏāh lipnê yiśrāʾēl wayyānusû ʾîš ləʾohālāyw — 'Judah was defeated before Israel, and every man fled to his own tent' (25:22). nāgap (H5062, to smite, defeat — used of YHWH striking enemies) is now used for Judah's defeat. The 'fleeing to his tent' idiom marks total rout (cf. 2 Sam 20:1; 1 Kgs 12:16). The Chronicler's brevity is its own commentary: the battle that Amaziah forced through pride ended in one sentence of collapse.
23: wəʾet ʾămatsyāhû melek yəhûḏāh ben-yôʾāš ben-yəhôʾāḥāz tāpas yôʾāš melek yiśrāʾēl bêt-šemeš — 'Joash king of Israel captured Amaziah king of Judah, the son of Joash, the son of Jehoahaz, at Beth-shemesh' (25:23). The genealogical identification (ben-chain) in the context of capture emphasizes the dynastic humiliation: a Davidic king is taken prisoner by an Israelite king. The capture of a Judean king by the northern kingdom is unprecedented in Chronicles and represents the nadir of Amaziah's reign.
24: wəʾet kol-hazāhāḇ wəhakkesef wəʾēt kol-hakkelîm hannimsāʾîm bêt hāʾĕlōhîm ʿim-ʿōḇēḏ ʾĕḏôm wəʾet-ʾôṣərôt bêt hammelek wəʾet bənê hattaʿărubôt wayyāšāḇ šōmərônāh — 'He took all the gold and silver and all the vessels found in the house of God with Obed-edom, the treasuries of the royal palace, and hostages; then he returned to Samaria' (25:24). ʿim-ʿōḇēḏ ʾĕḏôm (with Obed-edom) — the treasures are specifically located with Obed-edom, the Levitical family responsible for the ark's temporary housing (1 Chr 13:13-14; 15:18). The plunder of these Levitical treasuries underscores the sacred dimension of the loss.
25: wayyəḥî ʾămatsyāhû ben-yôʾāš melek yəhûḏāh ʾaḥărê môt yôʾāš ben-yəhôʾāḥāz melek yiśrāʾēl ḥămēš ʿeśrēh šānāh — 'Amaziah son of Joash king of Judah lived fifteen years after the death of Joash son of Jehoahaz king of Israel' (25:25). The survival note establishes the timeline: Amaziah outlived his captor by fifteen years, reigning on as a politically diminished king. The Chronicler's inclusion of this — identical to 2 Kgs 14:17 — frames the long reign (29 years total) as survival rather than flourishing. Longevity without covenant fidelity is existence, not blessing.
26: wəyeter diḇrê ʾămatsyāhû hārîʾšōnîm wəhāʾaḥărônîm halōʾ-hinnām kəṯûḇîm ʿal-sēper malḵê yəhûḏāh wəyiśrāʾēl — 'The rest of the acts of Amaziah, from first to last, are they not written in the Book of the Kings of Judah and Israel?' (25:26). The Chronicler's archival citation formula (kəṯûḇîm ʿal-sēper malḵê) appears after every regnal summary. The dual reference ('Judah and Israel') reflects a pan-Israelite archival tradition that the Chronicler draws on and that 2 Kings employs separately for each kingdom.
27: ûmēʿēt ʾăšer-sār ʾămatsyāhû mēʾaḥărê YHWH wayyiqšərû ʿālāyw qeser bîrûšālaim wayyānos lākîš — 'From the time Amaziah turned away from YHWH, they conspired against him in Jerusalem, and he fled to Lachish' (25:27). sār mēʾaḥărê YHWH (turned away from after YHWH) — the Chronicler's technical phrase for apostasy: not merely sin but directional departure. Lachish was a fortified royal city, but the flight to it signals political collapse: Amaziah cannot even remain in Jerusalem, the city of David.
28: wayyišlăḥû ʾaḥărāyw lākîš wayyimîtuhû šām wayyiśśāʾuhû ʿal-hasSûsîm wayyiqbərû ʾōtô ʿim-ʾăḇōṯāyw bəʿîr yəhûḏāh — 'They sent after him to Lachish and killed him there. They brought him back on horses and buried him with his fathers in the city of Judah' (25:28). The assassination of a fleeing king and his return for burial 'with his fathers' forms the Chronicler's standard regnal close. The burial formula (wayyiqbərû ʿim-ʾăḇōṯāyw) is granted even to failed kings, marking the persistence of the Davidic dynastic promise through individual royal failure.
